title: Deuteronomy 7
subtitle: Driving Out the Nations: Holy People, Uncompromising Separation

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — No Compromise with Canaan; the Holy People

heb.paragraph: Ḥāram means to dedicate something irrevocably to God — in this case for total destruction. The Canaanite herem is not ethnic but theological: contamination prevention. The rationale: intermarriage will lead to idolatry (v.4).

Qadosh (holy) means set apart, distinct, consecrated. Israel is "a holy people to the LORD your God" — not because of their size or virtue (v.7) but because of God's election and love. Holiness is a gift before it is a demand.

refs.note: "But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession." Peter applies Deut 7:6's election language to the church — the NT Israel is a "holy nation" by new-covenant inclusion.

"Do not be yoked together with unbelievers... 'Come out from them and be separate, says the Lord.'" Paul applies the principle of Deut 7's separation to the church's relationship with idolatrous culture.

notes.note: MacArthur: The prohibition of intermarriage with Canaanite peoples is not ethnic prejudice — it is spiritual self-preservation. Solomon's violation of exactly this command (1 Kgs 11:1–4) proves Moses right: his foreign wives "turned his heart after other gods." Deuteronomy 7 is not about racial purity; it is about covenant integrity.

MacArthur: "The LORD did not set his affection on you and choose you because you were more numerous than other peoples — for you were the fewest. But it was because the LORD loved you." This is election in its purest form: unconditional, personal, sovereign. Nothing in Israel merited God's love. The love came first and created the people it loved. This is the theological foundation of all grace.

MacArthur: "Know therefore that the LORD your God is God; he is the faithful God, keeping his covenant of love to a thousand generations of those who love him." Divine faithfulness is the ground of human faithfulness. The thousand-generation timeframe of his covenant love dwarfs any human timeframe. Israel's faithfulness is called forth by and modelled on God's own.

MacArthur: The blessings promised for covenant faithfulness — children, crops, cattle, health — are not health-and-wealth theology. They are the natural fruit of a society ordered according to God's design. Creation was designed to flourish under the stewardship of covenant humanity.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The seven nations list is a conventional formula for the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan. The herem command applies to the theological category — peoples whose religious practices posed an existential threat to Israel's covenant identity.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: On no compromise with Canaan: the JPS commentary observes that this legislation reflects the distinctive Deuteronomic concern for centralisation and humanitarian ethics. Where earlier law codes state the rule, Deuteronomy adds motivation clauses that ground the law in Israel's experience of God.

Tigay: Comparative ANE evidence illuminates the background — similar provisions appear in the Code of Hammurabi and the Middle Assyrian Laws, but Deuteronomy's theological framing is unique: the law serves the covenant relationship, not merely social order.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section reflects the characteristic Deuteronomic style — parenetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

NET Note: Several terms in this passage carry technical covenant vocabulary. The repeated use ofšāmar("to keep, guard") frames obedience as vigilant custody of God's commands, not passive acquiescence.

Calvin: On no compromise with Canaan: the law here is not mere legislation but divine pedagogy — God shapes his people's character through specific commands that address the conditions they will face in Canaan. Every statute reflects both God's holiness and his care for human wellbeing.

Calvin: The application extends to the church in every age. What Moses commands Israel in historical particularity, the Spirit applies to believers in principle. The form changes; the underlying demand for covenant faithfulness does not.

hist.context: Chapter 7 addresses the herem applied to the seven Canaanite nations. Moses gives three rationales: (1) contamination — intermarriage will lead to idolatry; (2) election — God chose Israel not because of size or merit but out of love; (3) faithfulness — God keeps covenant with those who love him. The command is not racial genocide but a theological surgical procedure: removing the cancer of Canaanite religion before it destroys Israel's covenant identity.

sections.header: Verses 17–26 — Do Not Be Afraid; God Will Drive Them Out

heb.gloss: do not be afraid of them
heb.paragraph: The fear prohibition is Deuteronomy's repeated pastoral refrain. The wilderness generation was disqualified by fear at Kadesh. "Do not be afraid" is not a denial of the enemy's power but a reorientation: the enemy's power is real, but God's power is infinitely greater.

refs.note: "For the Spirit God gave us does not make us timid, but gives us power, love and self-discipline." Paul's antidote to fear echoes Deuteronomy 7's repeated "do not be afraid" — both are grounded in the character and power of God.

notes.note: MacArthur: "You may say to yourselves, 'These nations are stronger than we are. How can we drive them out?' But do not be afraid of them; remember well what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt." Memory is Moses' primary weapon against fear. The antidote to the fear of overwhelming opposition is meditation on God's demonstrated power.

MacArthur: "The LORD your God will drive out those nations before you, little by little." The gradual conquest is explicitly providential — not a failure of divine power but an act of divine wisdom. Trusting God's timing means accepting that his schedule serves purposes we cannot always see.

MacArthur: The prohibition on keeping the gold and silver of idols is an anti-materialism command with theological bite. "Do not covet the silver and gold on them... it is detestable to the LORD." Achan's sin in Joshua 7 is the precise violation Moses is anticipating here.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: On do not fear; God will drive them out: the JPS commentary observes that this legislation reflects the distinctive Deuteronomic concern for centralisation and humanitarian ethics. Where earlier law codes state the rule, Deuteronomy adds motivation clauses that ground the law in Israel's experience of God.

Tigay: Comparative ANE evidence illuminates the background — similar provisions appear in the Code of Hammurabi and the Middle Assyrian Laws, but Deuteronomy's theological framing is unique: the law serves the covenant relationship, not merely social order.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: On do not fear; God will drive them out: the structure here follows the pattern of ANE treaty stipulations — specific obligations laid on the vassal by the suzerain. The parallels with Hittite treaty forms confirm that Deuteronomy's legal material is presented in a recognised international diplomatic format.

Craigie: The literary placement of this section within the larger address serves a rhetorical function — Moses builds from general principles to specific applications, a technique well attested in ancient Near Eastern legal and wisdom literature.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section reflects the characteristic Deuteronomic style — parenetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

NET Note: Several terms in this passage carry technical covenant vocabulary. The repeated use ofšāmar("to keep, guard") frames obedience as vigilant custody of God's commands, not passive acquiescence.

Calvin: On do not fear; God will drive them out: the law here is not mere legislation but divine pedagogy — God shapes his people's character through specific commands that address the conditions they will face in Canaan. Every statute reflects both God's holiness and his care for human wellbeing.

Calvin: The application extends to the church in every age. What Moses commands Israel in historical particularity, the Spirit applies to believers in principle. The form changes; the underlying demand for covenant faithfulness does not.

hist.context: Moses anticipates the objection: what if the nations are too numerous? The answer: remember what God did to Pharaoh and Egypt. He will drive out the nations "little by little" (v.22) — not all at once, because the land needs time to be inhabited and wild animals would multiply in empty territory. The gradualness is providential wisdom, not delay. The chapter closes with instructions on destroying idols — even the silver and gold of their images must not be kept.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Joshua↗ People
ppl.role: Moses’s military commander and designated successor
ppl.text: Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.

ppl.name: Og↗ People
ppl.role: Giant king of Bashan; last of the Rephaim
ppl.text: Og king of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim — a pre-Israelite giant people. His iron bed, measuring nine cubits long (c.4.5m), was preserved in Rabbah of Ammon as a monument (Deut 3:11). Israel defeated him at Edrei after his defeat of Sihon, and Moses divided his territory among the Transjordan tribes. Deut 3 presents his defeat as proof that God can give Israel the whole land: if the giant Og fell, so will the Anakites in Canaan. He appears in numerous later biblical summaries of God’s power (Ps 135, 136; Neh 9).

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (14th–13th c. BC)
src.quote: Klaus Baltzer (1960) and Meredith Kline (1963) demonstrated that Deuteronomy follows the six-part structure of Hittite vassal treaties: preamble (1:1–5), historical prologue (1:6–3:29), stipulations (4–26), document clause (27:1–9), witnesses (31:19–22), and blessings/curses (28). This structure is attested only in 14th–13th century BC treaties, strongly supporting Mosaic authorship.
src.note: The treaty-form evidence is the most powerful external argument for Deuteronomy’s early date. Subsequent Assyrian vassal treaties (7th c. BC) have a different form, making a late (Josian) dating of the book structurally improbable.

src.title: Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC)
src.quote: The Neo-Assyrian VTE contains curse language strikingly similar to Deut 28, leading some scholars (Weinfeld, Steymans) to argue Deuteronomy was modelled on Assyrian treaties. However, the VTE represents only the curse section; Deuteronomy’s full six-part structure matches only Hittite treaties.
src.note: The parallel demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern covenant forms were widespread, but the direction of dependence is disputed. The Hittite parallel is structurally closer and temporally earlier, supporting the traditional dating.

src.title: Ugaritic and Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Terminology
src.quote: The vocabulary of Deuteronomy —berît(covenant),ahavah(love as covenant loyalty),hesed(loyal love), ‘adon’ (lord) — is attested in Ugaritic and other ANE diplomatic texts as standard covenant terminology.
src.note: This shows that Deuteronomy’s language of love, loyalty, and service was not merely personal piety but carried legal-diplomatic weight: Israel’s relationship with God is structured as a formal suzerainty covenant.

rec.who: Origen, Homilies on Deuteronomy (3rd c. AD)
rec.text: "Origen read Deuteronomy christologically throughout, interpreting Moses’ farewell discourses as prefiguring Christ’s teaching, the “prophet like Moses” (18:15) as a direct prophecy of Christ, and the land as a figure of the soul’s heavenly inheritance."

"MacArthur: The prohibition of intermarriage with Canaanite peoples is not ethnic prejudice — it is spiritual self-preservation. Solomon's violation of exactly this command (1 Kgs 11:1–4) proves Moses …"

Qadosh (holy) means set apart, distinct, consecrated. Israel is "a holy people to the LORD your God" — not because of their size or virtue (v.7) but because of God's election and love. Holiness is a gift before it is a demand.

hebtext.note: The fear prohibition is Deuteronomy's repeated pastoral refrain. The wilderness generation was disqualified by fear at Kadesh. "Do not be afraid" is not a denial of the enemy's power but a reorientation: the enemy's power is real, but God's power is infinitely greater.

"Do not be yoked together with unbelievers... 'Come out from them and be separate, says the Lord.'" Paul applies the principle of Deut 7's separation to the church's relationship with idolatrous culture.

tx.title: LXX Deuteronomy and the MT divergences
tx.content: MTandLXXDeuteronomy diverge more significantly than other Pentateuchal books. The LXX adds explanatory expansions, especially in the blessings and curses (ch.28) and the Song of Moses (ch.32).
tx.note: The LXX Deuteronomy was translated with greater theological freedom than other books. It often interprets rather than translates, and has shaped the NT’s use of Deuteronomy (Paul cites LXX Deut in Rom 10 and Gal 3).

tx.title: Qumran and the Samaritan Pentateuch
tx.content: Deuteronomy is the most-copied book at Qumran (29 manuscripts), showing its centrality to Second Temple Judaism. TheSPDeuteronomy adds a tenth commandment mandating an altar on Gerizim (Exod 20:17b insert), the central SP polemic.
tx.note: The Nash Papyrus (c.150–100 BC) preserves the Decalogue and Shema from Deuteronomy — the oldest surviving Hebrew manuscript of any biblical text. It shows a mixed text-type combining MT and LXX features.

positions.name: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.proponents: The dominant critical view since de Wette (1805) identifies the “book of the law” found in 2 Kgs 22 with Deuteronomy, placing its composition in Josiah’s reign (c.621 BC). Deuteronomy on this view was written to legitimise Josiah’s centralisation reforms.
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.name: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.proponents: The Hittite suzerainty treaty structure (attested only in 14th–13th c. BC) strongly supports early composition. The book’s internal claims (1:1, 31:9,22,24) and NT citations (Jesus cites Deuteronomy three times in the temptation: Matt 4:4,7,10) support Mosaic origin.
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

themes.note: Driving Out the Nations: Holy People, Uncompromising Separation

coaching.tip: 'The LORD did not set his affection on you because you were more numerous' (v. 7). Israel was chosen—not for size, virtue, or potential. Election was love, not merit. Grace preceded obedience; obedience didn't earn grace.

The 'treasured possession' language (v. 6, Hebrew segullāh) reappears in 1 Peter 2:9: 'a people belonging to God.' Peter applies Israel's election vocabulary directly to the Gentile church. The exclusivism of Deuteronomy 7 — no intermarriage, no treaties — finds its New Testament counterpart not in ethnic separation but in spiritual distinctiveness: 'do not be yoked together with unbelievers' (2 Cor 6:14).
'Little by little' God would drive out the nations (v. 22). Conquest wouldn't be instantaneous—wild animals would multiply in empty land. The pace served ecology. Patience wasn't weakness but wisdom.

The chapter moves from command (vv. 1-5) to motivation (vv. 6-11) to promise (vv. 12-26). This three-part structure — imperative, indicative, promissory — is Deuteronomy's standard rhetorical architecture. Obedience is never presented as raw duty; it is always sandwiched between identity ('you are holy') and assurance ('God will bless'). The motivation precedes the demand.
